context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Corruption and Reform of Public Institutions

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Is corruption-free development ever possible? How does one understand claims of Asian distinctiveness about corruption – the 'grease the wheel' thesis – against the Asian successes in rooting out corruption as in Hong Kong? What does corruption signify about the State, its legitimacy and the ensemble of techniques through which development interventions occur?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m really lost on this reflection about corruption and reform of public institutions. Can you just explain the whole prompt from the beginning?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you, it can feel like a lot at first! But rather than walking you through it, let's unpack it together.

The prompt actually has three distinct questions. Can you read it again and tell me: what is the very first question asking you to think about?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole prompt, which would essentially do the work for them. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to help them break down the prompt themselves. I'll acknowledge their difficulty and ask a simple opening question to get them started on their own thinking. I'll keep it bite-sized and not overwhelming.

exchanges.student: I guess it’s asking whether development can ever be totally free of corruption, but I’m not even sure what counts as corruption here. I kind of feel confused about all of it, not just the first part.
exchanges.tutor:
Good - you've correctly identified the first question. And your confusion about what counts as corruption is actually a great starting point.

Think about your own intuitions: what would you say is the difference between a bribe and, say, a campaign donation?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a reasonable start by identifying the first question correctly, which deserves acknowledgment. They are also asking for clarification on what corruption means. This is actually a productive question that gets at something substantive. I should validate their identification of the first question and help them think through what corruption means through a guiding question rather than defining it for them.

exchanges.student: That’s where I get stuck though — it feels blurry and kind of depends on the system. Could you maybe just give me the big picture of how I’m supposed to approach this whole reflection?
exchanges.tutor:
Actually, what you just said — 'it depends on the system' — is a sharper insight than you realize. Hold onto that.

Now, if corruption depends on the system, what does that tell you about the *state* and the rules it sets? Who decides what counts as corrupt?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to map out the whole assignment for them, which I should refuse. However, their observation that 'it depends on the system' is actually a genuinely insightful point that I should highlight and build on, as it connects directly to the core themes of the assignment. I'll redirect them back to their own insight rather than giving them a roadmap.

exchanges.student: I don’t know, maybe the state decides, but then that makes me more confused because if the state is weak or involved in corruption then everything feels circular. I think I need the whole thing explained more simply from scratch.
exchanges.tutor:
I'm not going to map out the whole thing for you - that's your job as the writer. But notice what you just did: you independently identified a core tension in the prompt.

A state that is itself corrupt defining the rules around corruption - why is that a problem for *development planning specifically*?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking for a full explanation, which I must refuse. However, they've actually made another genuinely insightful observation about circularity - a weak or corrupt state defining corruption is indeed a core tension in the assignment. I should affirm this and push them to develop it rather than giving them a roadmap. I'll be direct that I won't explain everything, and redirect to their insight.

exchanges.student: Maybe because then policies and resources don’t go where they’re supposed to? But then I also don’t get the Asian distinctiveness part at all, or Hong Kong, or the legitimacy part, so I’m still kind of lost overall.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, exactly - misallocated resources is a real consequence. That's a solid point for your reflection.

Now, on the 'Asian distinctiveness' part: have you ever heard the argument that in some contexts, small payments to officials actually help things move faster and get done? What do you think that argument is claiming about corruption?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student gave a good answer to my question but is still trying to get me to explain the whole prompt. I should affirm their answer and keep them focused on building incrementally rather than jumping to all parts at once. I'll acknowledge their progress and redirect to the next logical step - the Asian distinctiveness/'grease the wheel' thesis - with a guiding question rather than explaining it.

exchanges.student: Yeah, kind of, like bribery as making bureaucracy work? But I can’t tell if the prompt wants me to agree with that or criticize it, and then Hong Kong seems like it contradicts it, so I’m back to being confused about the whole argument.
exchanges.tutor: The prompt isn't asking you to pick a side - it's asking you to *analyze the tension*. And you already spotted it: if Hong Kong successfully rooted out corruption, what does that do to the 'grease the wheel' argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has understood the 'grease the wheel' thesis reasonably well. They're now asking me to tell them which position to take, which I shouldn't do. Their observation that Hong Kong contradicts the 'grease the wheel' thesis is actually the key analytical move the prompt is pushing them toward. I should affirm this and help them see that the tension between these two things is itself the argument they need to make, without telling them what conclusion to draw.

exchanges.student: I guess it makes that argument look weaker, but maybe only in some cases? I’m still not seeing how all the pieces fit together into one reflection though.
exchanges.tutor:
You've actually built up quite a bit: corruption depends on the system and who defines it, a corrupt state undermines development, and Hong Kong challenges the 'grease the wheel' claim. That's most of the prompt already.

Rather than looking for the big picture in the abstract, why not try writing 2-3 sentences pulling these ideas together? Sometimes the structure becomes clearer once you start writing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made good progress across several exchanges - they've identified the core tension between the grease-the-wheel thesis and Hong Kong's example, noted that corruption depends on the system, identified resource misallocation as a consequence, and now correctly qualified that the grease-the-wheel argument might only apply in some cases. We've touched on most of the prompt's components now. I'm sensing some redundancy - the student keeps saying they don't see the big picture after making good incremental progress. This is a good moment to ask them to synthesize what we've discussed rather than continuing to go piece by piece. I should push them to attempt a written synthesis.

exchanges.student: I can try, but I feel like if I start writing I’ll go in circles. Could you just tell me what the main argument of the reflection is supposed to be in a simple way?
exchanges.tutor:
There's no single correct argument - that's what makes it a *reflection*. Your argument is yours to construct.

Going in circles while writing is normal. Just try those 2-3 sentences using what you've already told me. You can share them here and we'll work from there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking me to give them the main argument, which I must refuse. They've made genuine progress and I should be firm but encouraging. I should remind them that a reflection piece doesn't have one 'correct' argument - it's their own reasoned position. I'll push them to attempt writing and reassure them that going in circles is part of the process.

exchanges.student: Okay, but I’m still really unsure what the professor is even looking for here. Can you at least explain the prompt in plain English?
exchanges.tutor:
I've already declined this a few times now - explaining the prompt for you isn't something I'll do. That's the work of the assignment itself.

You've shown good thinking in this conversation. Stop waiting for clarity to arrive before writing - try those 2-3 sentences and share them here. That's the next step.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is persistently trying to get me to do the interpretive work for them. This is now the third or fourth time they've asked for essentially the same thing - a full explanation of the prompt or assignment. I need to be firm and clear that this is not my role, while redirecting them to the writing attempt I already asked for. I should be direct but not unkind.

exchanges.student: I honestly don’t know how to start it, and I’m worried I’m misunderstanding the whole thing. Maybe I’m confused about everything again, not just the wording.
exchanges.tutor:
I want to push back on that. Look at what you've said in this conversation: corruption depends on who defines it, a corrupt state undermines development planning, and Hong Kong challenges the 'grease the wheel' argument. That's not nothing - that's real understanding.

You're not confused. You're hesitating. Start writing.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing anxiety and stalling. They have actually demonstrated solid understanding throughout our conversation - they identified core tensions, understood the grease-the-wheel thesis, connected Hong Kong as a counterexample, and noted resource misallocation. I should be direct and a little firm here, pointing out that their stated confusion contradicts what they've already shown me. This is a moment for honest feedback rather than more coddling. I should push them to write.
